_meta.description: Beyond Paradise sourced fact database. Every claim on the site must trace back to an entry here with ≥2 independent sources. Mirrors WildToSea facts.json schema.
_meta.rules: 2+ independent sources required for status=confirmed. Single source = single-source. Never publish a specific number without a confirmed entry.

facts.claim: Tanzania tourist visa cost
facts.value: USD 50 single-entry for most nationalities. USD 100 for US citizens (multiple-entry, 12 months). Apply online at visa.immigration.go.tz or on arrival at JRO/DAR/ZNZ for eligible nationalities.

facts.claim: Zanzibar mandatory inbound travel insurance requirement
facts.value: Mandatory since October 2024: all non-resident visitors must purchase the official ZIC inbound insurance at visitzanzibar.go.tz before arrival. USD 44/adult, USD 22/child (3–17 years), free under 3. QR receipt checked at Zanzibar immigration. Other travel insurance policies not accepted.

facts.claim: Kenya e-Visa cost
facts.value: USD 50 single-entry e-Visa required for most nationalities. Apply at evisa.go.ke. East African Community citizens exempt. East African Tourist Visa (Tanzania + Kenya + Uganda combined) available for USD 100.

facts.claim: Vietnam e-Visa cost and validity
facts.value: USD 25. 90-day multiple-entry e-Visa available online at evisa.xuatnhapcanh.gov.vn. Covers all entry points. Most EU, US, UK, Australian passport holders eligible. Processing: 3 business days.

facts.claim: Yellow fever requirement Tanzania
facts.value: Yellow fever certificate required only if arriving from (or transiting >12h in) a yellow fever endemic country. Not required for direct arrivals from Europe, North America, or Australia. Certificate is valid for life.

facts.claim: Malaria risk Zanzibar
facts.value: Malaria is present in Zanzibar year-round. Prophylaxis (Malarone/doxycycline/mefloquine) strongly recommended. Risk higher in rainy seasons (Mar–May, Oct–Nov). Atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone) preferred for Zanzibar — good efficacy, minimal side effects.

facts.claim: Whale shark season at Mafia Island
facts.value: Whale sharks aggregate around Mafia Island from October to February, with peak sightings November to January. This aggregation is one of the largest documented in the Indian Ocean.

facts.claim: Number of individually identified whale sharks in Tanzania waters
facts.value: Approximately 180–200 individual whale sharks have been photo-identified in Tanzania waters using fin and spot patterns via the Wildbook for Whale Sharks (sharkbook.ai) citizen science database.

facts.claim: Whale shark IUCN conservation status
facts.value: Endangered (EN) on the IUCN Red List since 2016. Global population declining due to vessel strikes, fisheries bycatch, and plastic ingestion. Rhincodon typus.

facts.claim: Regulations for swimming with whale sharks at Mafia Marine Park
facts.value: Maximum 6 swimmers per individual whale shark at any time. Minimum 3m distance from body, 4m from tail. No touching. No underwater flash photography. Vessels must cut engine when within 50m of animal. Permit required from Marine Parks and Reserves Unit Tanzania.

facts.claim: Dolphin species in Menai Bay Conservation Area
facts.value: Two resident species: Indo-Pacific bottlenose dolphin (Tursiops aduncus) and spinner dolphin (Stenella longirostris), typically encountered in mixed groups of 200–400 individuals.

facts.claim: Tourism impact on Menai Bay dolphins
facts.value: A 2016 PLOS ONE peer-reviewed study (Amir et al.) documented significant behavioural disruption to Menai Bay dolphins from boat-based tourism. Dolphins showed avoidance behaviour when multiple boats were present. MBCA guidelines (boat limits, engine-off rules) exist but enforcement is inconsistent.

facts.claim: Sea turtle species found in Zanzibar waters
facts.value: Five IUCN-listed sea turtle species are found in Tanzanian coastal waters: green turtle (Chelonia mydas, EN), hawksbill turtle (Eretmochelys imbricata, CR), loggerhead turtle (Caretta caretta, VU), leatherback turtle (Dermochelys coriacea, VU), and olive ridley turtle (Lepidochelys olivacea, VU). Green and hawksbill are most commonly encountered at Zanzibar.

facts.claim: Sea turtle nesting season Mnemba Island
facts.value: Sea turtles nest on Mnemba Island beaches October to February. andBeyond Mnemba Island partners with the School for Field Studies on sea turtle monitoring.

facts.claim: Thanda Island sea turtle conservation programme
facts.value: Thanda Island launched an active nest protection programme in 2017. First documented hatchlings recorded May 2017. In 2023, 4 nests were recorded with 100+ hatchlings. Five IUCN-listed sea turtle species documented in surrounding waters.

facts.claim: Coral reef health on Pemba Island
facts.value: CORDIO East Africa 2024 survey: average hard coral cover on Pemba reefs is 26%. Only 2 of 12 surveyed sites exceed 40% hard coral cover. East and south coasts have strong currents — experienced divers only. Western coast more accessible.

facts.claim: Mnemba Atoll coral bleaching history
facts.value: Mnemba Atoll experienced significant coral bleaching in 2016 (Indian Ocean mass bleaching event). Reef restoration efforts ongoing. Current reef health shows recovery in some zones but permanent damage in others. Snorkelling quality varies significantly by site — ask operators which zones are currently active.

facts.claim: Wildebeest population in Great Migration
facts.value: Approximately 1.5 million wildebeest, 200,000 zebra, and 350,000 Thomson's gazelle participate in the annual Great Migration circuit across the Serengeti-Mara ecosystem.

facts.claim: Calving season wildebeest statistics
facts.value: During the Serengeti calving season (January–February, Ndutu area), approximately 400,000 calves are born over a 3-week window. At peak, up to 8,000 calves are born per day. This concentrated birth event triggers the highest predator density recorded in Africa.

facts.claim: Lion population in Serengeti ecosystem
facts.value: The Serengeti-Mara ecosystem supports an estimated 3,000+ lions, one of the largest contiguous lion populations in Africa.

facts.claim: African lion IUCN conservation status
facts.value: Vulnerable (VU) on the IUCN Red List. African lion population declined by approximately 43% over 21 years (3 generations). Estimated 20,000–25,000 individuals remain in the wild.

facts.claim: Grumeti River crossing timing
facts.value: Wildebeest begin crossing the Grumeti River in the Serengeti Western Corridor from late May to August, with peak crossings in June–July. The Grumeti River hosts large Nile crocodile populations that make crossings particularly dramatic.

facts.claim: Great Migration arrival in Masai Mara
facts.value: The first wildebeest herds typically cross into Kenya's Masai Mara from Tanzania in late July, with peak crossings at the Mara River in August–September. Herds begin returning south in October–November.

facts.claim: Singita Grumeti private concession size
facts.value: Singita Grumeti operates within a 350,000-acre (142,000 hectare) private wildlife concession in the Serengeti Western Corridor. The concession is managed by the Grumeti Fund for anti-poaching and conservation.

facts.claim: Mnemba Island Lodge maximum guest capacity
facts.value: andBeyond Mnemba Island has 12 bandas (beachfront chalets), accommodating a maximum of 24 guests. This private island exclusivity means no day visitors from mainland Zanzibar.

facts.claim: Mahali Mzuri tent count and capacity
facts.value: Mahali Mzuri has 12 tented suites across the Olare Motorogi Conservancy, Kenya. Maximum 24 guests. The conservancy allows off-road game driving, unlike the public Masai Mara National Reserve.

facts.claim: Zanzibar climate seasons
facts.value: Zanzibar has two rainy seasons: long rains (Masika) March–May, and short rains (Vuli) October–November. Best months for marine activities: October–March when Indian Ocean is calm and visibility is highest. SE trade winds (Kusi) June–September bring choppy east coast seas but excellent kitesurf conditions at Paje.

facts.claim: Kitesurfing season at Paje, Zanzibar
facts.value: Paje beach on Zanzibar's east coast is the premier kitesurfing location in East Africa. SE trade winds (Kusi) blow consistently June–September at 18–25 knots. The flat lagoon at Paje is suitable for beginners to advanced riders.

facts.claim: Serengeti dry season dates
facts.value: Serengeti dry season runs June–October. This is peak safari season: short grass improves wildlife visibility, animals concentrate around water sources, and roads are passable throughout. Long rains: March–May (some lodges close, northern roads can flood). Short rains: November (brief, usually doesn't disrupt game drives).

facts.claim: Masai Mara National Reserve park fees
facts.value: Non-resident adult park fees: USD 80/person/day in peak season (July–September), USD 70/person/day in low season. Conservancy fees additional: USD 80–120/person/night depending on conservancy (Olare Motorogi, Mara North, etc.). Most luxury camps include both in their all-inclusive rate.

facts.claim: Serengeti National Park non-resident fees
facts.value: Non-resident adult fee: USD 70/person/day for Serengeti National Park. Ngorongoro Conservation Area fee: USD 70/person/day additional (if visiting crater). Most luxury lodges include park fees in their all-inclusive rates.

facts.claim: Mafia Marine Park entry fee
facts.value: USD 20/person/day to enter Mafia Marine Park. Whale shark interaction permit included with MPRU-registered operators. Scuba diving requires separate dive fees paid to the operator.

facts.claim: International flight connections to Zanzibar (ZNZ)
facts.value: No direct flights from Europe to Zanzibar. Best connections: Nairobi (NBO) via Kenya Airways/Jambojet (45 min), Dar es Salaam (DAR) via Precision Air/Air Tanzania (20 min), Dubai (DXB) via flydubai (direct, ~5h). Qatar Airways and Ethiopian Airlines also connect via their hubs.

facts.claim: Flight time from Nairobi to Masai Mara
facts.value: Scheduled flights from Nairobi Wilson Airport (WIL) to Masai Mara airstrips (Kichwa Tembo, Ol Kiombo, Governor's): 45–60 minutes. Daily scheduled flights with AirKenya and SafariLink. Charter also available.

facts.claim: Flight connections to Serengeti
facts.value: Charter and scheduled flights from Arusha Airport (ARK) or Kilimanjaro (JRO) to Serengeti airstrips (Seronera, Grumeti, Lamai, Kogatende, Lobo): 45 minutes to 1.5 hours depending on destination. Coastal Aviation and Auric Air offer scheduled service. No road access for most ultra-luxury camps.

facts.claim: Best travel season for Vietnam
facts.value: Vietnam's climate varies dramatically by region. Hoi An and central coast: best November–April (dry, 25–30°C). Hanoi and north: best October–April (cooler, drier). Ho Chi Minh City and south: year-round, driest November–April. Avoid central Vietnam September–October (typhoon risk). No single 'best month' covers all regions.

facts.claim: Luxury hotel options in Hoi An
facts.value: Top luxury properties near Hoi An: Four Seasons Resort Nam Hai (15 minutes from Old Town, beach resort), Anantara Hoi An Resort (on Thu Bon River, in town), La Siesta Premium (boutique, Old Town). From €300–1,200/night depending on property and season.

facts.claim: Mountain gorilla permit cost Rwanda
facts.value: USD 1,500 per person for a 1-hour mountain gorilla trekking permit in Volcanoes National Park, Rwanda. Permits limited and must be booked in advance through Rwanda Development Board (rdb.rw). Only 8 people permitted per gorilla group per day.

facts.claim: Mountain gorilla world population
facts.value: Mountain gorillas (Gorilla beringei beringei) have a total world population of approximately 1,063 individuals as of the 2018 census (most recent comprehensive survey). The Virunga Massif (spanning Rwanda, DRC, Uganda) supports about 604 individuals.

facts.claim: Bisate Lodge conservation credentials
facts.value: andBeyond Bisate Lodge in Rwanda's Volcanoes National Park runs an active reforestation programme on the lodge site (former farmland returned to Afromontane forest). 50% of conservation fee goes directly to Rwandan national park authority. Lodge design uses local artisans and volcanic stone.

facts.claim: Singita Grumeti Fund conservation activities
facts.value: The Grumeti Fund (Singita's conservation arm) operates a ranger canine unit and anti-poaching patrol covering the 350,000-acre concession, a community programme serving 50+ villages in the buffer zone (schools, clinics, income-generation), and a wildlife monitoring programme tracking lion, leopard, elephant, and cheetah populations.

facts.claim: Off-road driving rules in Masai Mara
facts.value: Off-road driving is NOT permitted in the Masai Mara National Reserve (public area). It IS permitted in private conservancies bordering the reserve: Olare Motorogi Conservancy (Mahali Mzuri), Mara North Conservancy, Naboisho Conservancy. This is the single most important reason to choose a conservancy camp over a reserve-adjacent camp.

facts.claim: Stone Town UNESCO World Heritage status
facts.value: Stone Town (Zanzibar City) was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2000. The inscription covers the historic town's unique Swahili architecture, Arab and Indian merchant houses, carved wooden doors, and multicultural heritage as a former Omani Sultanate capital and Indian Ocean trade hub.

facts.claim: Ha Long Bay UNESCO status
facts.value: Ha Long Bay was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994 (natural values) and again in 2000 (aesthetic values extension). The bay contains approximately 1,600 islands and islets, most uninhabited. Luxury overnight cruises typically include Bai Tu Long Bay (fewer boats) or Ha Long Bay itself.

facts.claim: Hue Imperial Citadel UNESCO status
facts.value: The Complex of Hue Monuments was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1993. The Imperial Citadel (Kinh Thanh) was the seat of the Nguyen Dynasty (1802–1945), Vietnam's last imperial family. Entry: VND 200,000 (~USD 8) for Citadel; separate fees for royal tombs outside the walls.

facts.claim: Humpback whale passage timing off Tanzania coast
facts.value: Southern Ocean humpback whales (Megaptera novaeangliae) migrate north along the East African coast between July and September annually. Sightings are possible from boats along the southern Tanzania coast (Kilwa Masoko to Mtwara region). Whale watching is not commercially established in Tanzania — encounters are opportunistic.

facts.claim: Swimming with humpback whales — where permitted
facts.value: Swimming with humpback whales in the wild is legally permitted only in a few locations globally: Tonga (Vava'u, Ha'apai), Silver Bank (Dominican Republic, strictly regulated), Cook Islands. Not established or regulated in Tanzania. Vessel approach: minimum 100m distance recommended under IWC guidelines for all locations.

facts.claim: Best time for cheetah sightings in Serengeti
facts.value: Cheetah are resident year-round in the Serengeti, with highest sighting density in the southern plains (Ndutu, Naabi) during the calving season (January–March) when prey is abundant and grass is short. Serengeti supports approximately 1,000 cheetah — one of the world's highest densities.

facts.claim: Best time for leopard sightings in Serengeti
facts.value: Leopards are resident year-round in the Serengeti, most reliably spotted in the Seronera valley woodlands (Central Serengeti) and in the rocky kopjes throughout. Dry season (June–October) offers better visibility due to shorter grass. Kopje areas are productive year-round.

facts.claim: Mountain gorilla trekking rules
facts.value: Maximum 1 hour with gorilla family once located. Minimum approach distance: 7m (masks must be worn within 10m). Maximum 8 visitors per gorilla group per day. Children under 15 not permitted on gorilla treks. Trekking can take 1–6 hours depending on where the gorillas have moved overnight.

facts.claim: Best dive sites in Zanzibar
facts.value: Top dive sites: Mnemba Atoll (protected marine reserve, excellent reef fish, turtles, dolphins; access restricted to guests and registered operators), Leven Bank (30km offshore, open water, sharks), Tumbatu Island (wall dive, large Napoleon wrasse), Pemba Island (wall diving, pristine reefs, liveaboard access). Best dive season: October–March (calm seas, 25–30m visibility).

facts.claim: Con Dao Islands Vietnam overview
facts.value: Con Dao Islands (130km south of Ho Chi Minh City) form Vietnam's most biodiverse marine national park. Sea turtle nesting beaches (green and hawksbill), pristine reefs, and only ~10,000 annual visitors make it Vietnam's most exclusive destination. Access: 45-minute flight from Ho Chi Minh City (SGN) or 12h overnight ferry.

facts.claim: Six Senses Con Dao overview
facts.value: Six Senses Con Dao on Con Son Island is widely regarded as Vietnam's best resort. 50 private pool villas on a beach fronting the national park. June–September: sea turtle nesting on adjacent beach (guided night walks with rangers). From USD 700–1,200/villa/night in peak season.

facts.claim: Four Seasons Resort Nam Hai overview
facts.value: Four Seasons Resort The Nam Hai is located on Ha My Beach (Hoi An province), a 15-minute drive from Hoi An Ancient Town. 100 private pool villas. Three pools including an Olympic-length lap pool. Rated as one of Vietnam's finest beach resorts. From USD 650–1,500/villa/night.

facts.claim: Sofitel Legend Metropole Hanoi overview
facts.value: The Sofitel Legend Metropole Hanoi opened in 1901 and is Vietnam's most storied colonial hotel. The Metropole Wing (original 1901 building) has 244 rooms. Notable guests include Graham Greene, Charlie Chaplin, and Jane Fonda (who sheltered in the hotel's WWII bomb shelter, open for tours). From USD 300–600/night, Metropole Wing.

facts.claim: Ngorongoro Crater size and wildlife
facts.value: The Ngorongoro Crater is the world's largest intact volcanic caldera at 260 km² (100 square miles). It supports approximately 25,000 large mammals including the densest lion population in Africa, black rhino (endangered, critically important breeding group of ~30–40 individuals), elephant, hippo, flamingo, and wildebeest resident year-round.

facts.claim: Black rhino population in Ngorongoro Crater
facts.value: The Ngorongoro Conservation Area supports approximately 30–40 black rhinos (Diceros bicornis), one of the most important breeding populations in East Africa. Black rhinos are Critically Endangered (CR) on the IUCN Red List. Sightings in the Crater are not guaranteed — average sighting probability is approximately 50% per visit.

facts.claim: African wild dog presence in Serengeti
facts.value: African wild dogs (Lycaon pictus, Endangered) are present in the Serengeti but rarely seen. An estimated 30–40 individuals exist in the Serengeti ecosystem. Sightings are most likely in the northern Serengeti (Lamai area) October–December when packs denning. Rarest of the major predators to see.

facts.claim: African elephant population in Serengeti
facts.value: The Serengeti ecosystem supports approximately 8,000–9,000 African savannah elephants (Loxodonta africana, Vulnerable). Poaching was severe in the 1980s–1990s but populations have recovered substantially. Best elephant viewing: Seronera and Maswa areas year-round.

facts.claim: Michamvi Pingwe location and character
facts.value: Michamvi Pingwe is a small fishing village on Zanzibar's southeast coast, approximately 45km from Stone Town (1 hour drive). The peninsula offers calm lagoon waters on the west and open Indian Ocean on the east. This is the location of Hotel Matlai — Tim's base of operations.

facts.claim: Zanzibar spice history
facts.value: Zanzibar was the world's largest producer of cloves in the 19th century, earning the nickname 'Spice Island'. Cloves, nutmeg, cinnamon, pepper, and vanilla are still grown on Unguja Island (main Zanzibar island). Spice farm tours from Stone Town take approximately 3 hours.

facts.claim: Minimum realistic budget for East Africa luxury safari
facts.value: A realistic minimum for a genuine luxury East Africa safari (7 nights, one camp, all-in lodge + flights) is €6,000–8,000 per person. Budget includes: 1 week at a premium camp (€1,500–2,000/night/couple), internal charter flights (~€400–600 per flight), international connections (€600–1,200), and visas/entry fees. Ultra-luxury (Singita, Serian, andBeyond) doubles this.

facts.claim: andBeyond Bisate Lodge overview
facts.value: andBeyond Bisate Lodge sits in an extinct volcanic cone in Rwanda's Volcanoes National Park, 15 minutes from the park gate. 12 forest villas with direct views of the Virunga volcanoes. Active reforestation of 13 hectares of former farmland. From USD 3,000–4,000/person/night (includes gorilla permit, full-board, game drives). One of Africa's most conservation-forward lodges.

facts.claim: Nile crocodile population at Grumeti River
facts.value: The Grumeti River in the Serengeti Western Corridor has one of the highest concentrations of large Nile crocodiles (Crocodylus niloticus) in East Africa. These crocodiles can exceed 5 metres in length and are the main predator during wildebeest river crossings in June–August.

facts.claim: Flamingo numbers at Lake Manyara
facts.value: Lake Manyara supports large flamingo aggregations (both lesser and greater flamingo) when water levels and alkalinity are suitable. Numbers vary from thousands to over one million depending on conditions. Lake Manyara National Park also features tree-climbing lions (unique behaviour).

sources.name: Serengeti.com

facts.claim: The whale shark population in Mafia Island Marine Park increased from approximately 100 individuals in 2012 to 180 in December 2017, and further to 206 individuals in December 2019.

sources.name: WWF Tanzania
sources.quote: The population of whale sharks in Mafia Island Marine Park increased from approximately 100 individuals in 2012 to 180 individuals as of December 2017, according to WWF Tanzania. A report published by WWF Tanzania in October 2020 stated the population had increased to 206 individuals in December 2019.

This study, with Dr. Baraka Kuguru from the Tanzania Fisheries Research Institute, described it as a 'comparatively small population'.
